speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. The interview with Ester Lebensold. Today is November 27, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about the Lodz Ghetto. How did the Germans get people to move into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people ran into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They run to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of fear? There was a certain area where the Jews felt they would be safe, and other areas of the city were dangerous?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got-- they came to families, they came there, too. In the cemetery, they get covered in the cemetery. Any place, there or not, they run away because if not, that they killed so many people when they run.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you first went into the ghetto, you lived with your mother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what job did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was working in a factory. We made the clothes for the German. And after, I got a better job. We was making the-- like little purses for the pistols.

speaker: interviewer
text: Holsters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Holsters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first job you were making clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: For German soldiers. I was sewing fur because there was fighting on the Russian land. It was very cold. So we make the furs for them. And that's only by hand. I was- it's a special needle, three kinds. Oh, I learned so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you made these leather holsters, did you sew them by hand also?

speaker: interviewee
text: By hand. It's-- you sit on, like, a [INAUDIBLE] You sit on it, and here is the leather, and you made with two needles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did each of these places, these factories, have a lot of people working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all of them, all youngsters, all the people who was able to go. My mother was working at home. She made the-- from the-- pieces, she make the carpets, little carpets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: If not, you wouldn't get the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you didn't work, you didn't get any soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: Any soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Any food at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think every day. It was a special store. You have cards, and you went in, and you get food. So much and so much. Everything was very little. It was limited.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it? Was it soup and bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soup we got. In the factory, we get the soup and a piece of bread, I think, and-- in the factory. And in the town, we got a little bit sugar. Everything a little, a little bit. And we got our own money. The ghetto has their own money, with Rumkowski on the-- a picture from Rumkowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was working in a factory for leather, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leather also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother, who had the wife and three-year-old, he had a job? They had jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was-- he was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had to work also even with a three year old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yeah. And my sister, she was a forelady, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the German was wearing in the Russian on the boots from straw. Put on the boots to keep them warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she made them. She was the forelady in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your mother-in-law? Did she get a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-in-law, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send her-- they send her-- very early--

speaker: interviewer
text: They sent her away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people know where people were being sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But the end, when they liquidate the Lodz Ghetto, the main-- what's his name? Was-- oh my-- the German, he says they're sending us. We're going to have homes, and you should take everything what we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We believed in it. We didn't believe everything because you got people-- I don't know how it came out from Auschwitz-- they make from the people soap. And they make a lot of things from the skin from the people after they were was cremated. So we know a little bit. No, it was very hard to believe in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So early on, your mother-in-law was sent away from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- I don't-- I don't remember. They sent her very early, very early. She wasn't there the whole year. The whole-- no we were still there five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went back to live with your mother and father and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had just these two jobs the whole five years you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with Rumkowski at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got from Rumkowski-- that's a story. I-- when I lived with my mother-in-law, people, they came from-- from the city. Doctors-- it was a big building-- doctors and the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very high society. A lady was there that was very rich. They got a house in-- in Italy. They got a house in Germany. They-- there was-- they got a factory that was making the blankets. Very rich people. Her husband was in hospital, he got killed. And she came-- Mrs. Laskowski-- she came in the same building my mother-in-law used to live. And we get bread. We get sugar. And everything has to be-- somebody has to-- to make-- to keep-- to take care-- care on it. So they picked me because I got beautiful hands. Now they're old. They aren't beautiful. They picked me. So-- and she wasn't but only a month. And she fell in love with me. She likes me very much. And when they opened in the ghetto, they opened hotels for the people what there was working very hard. There-- they send them for two weeks, and they got everything over there. And she was the manager there. And she got the house near the-- the hotel. So she took me out from the factory, and she gave me a job, a waitress to work and-- and I got food. And I bring home food for my parents. Because there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people that were able to stay in the hotels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was three hotels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, three hotels. The people what they were working in the factory. They was working very hard, so they got vacation for two weeks. And she was the manager there. A beautiful lady, she could be alive. I beg her to come with me. Anyways, Rumkowski came time to time. He came to look around. And the old girls, the girls what they got a high school education, they didn't took girls plain. No, all of them, they got-- they finished high school. Beautiful girls with education, everything, they took them to work. And I got [INAUDIBLE] because she likes me, and she took me in. And I was staying with her when I was working there. But when Rumkowski has to come, a lot of girls has to run out into-- I was under the floor-- under the bed by her. He shouldn't find-- he shouldn't find us because he can take any girl that he want. He-- he just-- he was just like a king, Rumkowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he would do this to Jewish girls even though he was married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-

speaker: interviewee
text: He married a girl also, she's a lawyer. She would never marry him before the war. And he was the home what the people came to enjoy, you know, to forget a little bit in the hotel. And across was the court. And she was working across in the court, his wife, Rumkowski's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet he was after the pretty girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the girls, yeah. I was-- oh, she knew before because he has to let her know he's coming. Because she was the manager there. And she's says Ester, run to my house, and lay under-- under the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any girl that got taken by him? Or did everyone know to hide? Some girls actually got taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. He got like I harem-- a harem.

speaker: interviewer
text: A harem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So any time-- did this only happen at the hotel? Or did girls have to hide if he visited a factory, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the other places, I don't know. I was working there. And after the liquidate and I went to-- to make the-- with the leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: The holsters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to this lady when they liquidated the hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, when they liquidate the hotels, she was manager in hospital, and she want to take me there. And I could have a perfect job. I was too sensitive. If I have to take out from somebody, I would throw up. When I see on the street, somebody threw up, I threw right away. My mother says, if you go to the hospital, you're not-- you're not going to come out alive because you're not going to take it. And I didn't go. And I took the job with the leather. And when they liquidate the ghetto, I says please, Mrs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Laskowski, please come with me-- because her mother died-- please come with me. You took care for me. I will try to take on. And they let a few hundred people stay in the ghetto after they got killed. And she didn't know. They says they-- they would let them live. Her son lives in Israel, her only son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went and you were working in the leather factory. During this time, were they continuing to deport people from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, every few days, every few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they bring in people to the ghetto from other areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, from all around Lodz. From [INAUDIBLE], from-- from-- all around. Sure, if not, there wouldn't be nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they kept sending people out, but they kept bringing people in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right, from the small town, all around. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So as the years went by, did conditions in the ghetto get any worse, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't change.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't change.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing, what they take out people. No, but us, it was really something. We got the apartment downstairs. And when they make-- they-- they-- they-- it was still 5 o'clock was allowed to go out. 5 o'clock, everything was closed, and they came to pick people. Everybody has to go out and to stay in the line. And the younger people they take this way and the older people, the children, in the oven, they got killed, the older, the children. And my sister-in-law gave birth a day before to a little boy in our home. She was in bed. And the people were staying here by the window. And she was covered like a bed is made. And the baby wasn't crying. And we were staying outside and we were-- we were-- were scared to death. And my mother she was, at that time, in a very bad condition, and she was also covered in the house. And the SS men passed through to look at us, and they came out alive. They didn't catch my sister-in-law nor the baby. After three years, she has to give him up in Auschwitz. And she went with the baby because she didn't want to give him. She didn't know that they were going to put in crematorium, both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So periodically they would do this kind of selecting--

speaker: interviewee
text: All few months. All few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And try to get the old people and the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: The old people, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would they send the children away without  their parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were Jewish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish police did this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they help a lot because they want to stay alive. It's normal. Jewish Police and-- and the money was his picture. You got the ghetto money.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people feel about these Jewish police doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. Terrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In a time like that, nobody thinks about the other one. Everybody is selfish. Even you-- you wasn't selfish, you get selfish because they want to stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the general opinion that you-- among you and your friends about Rumkowski? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We get to-- we make theaters. And we-- and we got a lot of songs about Rumkowski. And it was-- we were-- even concentration camp, we came home after work, and we got a show in-- in the hall with the Hungarian girls and with-- they teach us Hungarian songs. And sometimes you have to forget. No, we got a lot of shows in the ghetto what we made from--

speaker: interviewer
text: People put on shows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh yes, and songs. A lot of songs. We have a song-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH]. It's when the Jews-- when Moses took the Jews and they have only cream of wheat to eat. And we have a song, [SINGING IN YIDDISH]. He was thinking, very long, to make a ghetto. He was-- he was thinking day and night to make the ghetto. [SINGING IN YIDDISH]. And he giving [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] is what they give the Jews when they passed the-- the-- the Red Ocean. That's all what they got is cream of wheat. So we made everything songs. We got a lot of songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do most of this while you were working, or after work you would-- they'd able to sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- we-- we went to the theater and we've got the-- the-- the--- Russian a little bit sugar-- I don't know what they get. We went to the theater with-- with everything It was-- for us, we got so used to it, it was normal. Yeah, for five years you have-- you have to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people in the ghetto write these plays and write these songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, it's a song-- it's not the same [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Every time when we have the memorial for high holidays, we have our cemetery. And we have a big monument from the Lodz Ghetto. And in the back, we have the names from our parents, from our brothers, little-- and every time we sing, [SINGING IN YIDDISH]. [SOBS] A man was writing in camp. And he says, it will come a day and we was-- we're going to live like peaches in heaven, [INAUDIBLE]. And that's a song we always sing when everything is finished. It goes [SINGING IN YIDDISH]. Don't say never. You go the last way. [INAUDIBLE] the heaven and the shining. And the-- the song is not written with the ink. It's written in blood and the gun is in the hand. The guns that the SS was holding the-- the sticks and there was-- there was heated gas. And the song goes, the song is not written with-- in ink. It's written with blood with the gun is in the hand. And the gun is-- is-- is what they--

speaker: interviewer
text: The whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: With whips. The song is-- I have it at home. And when I start to sing the song, I have to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it written-- it was in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish,yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Yiddish. These songs were in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The songs are Yiddish, and it came out from concentration camp for somebody--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had written it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Had written.

speaker: interviewer
text: The plays that they did in the ghetto, were they mostly in Yiddish also or were they in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything was done in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not allowed to speak Polish. If they hear when we'd speak Polish, they'd throw stones on us. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Put the-- the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans, did they want you to speak German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because, they was in the-- there was-- it was in the ghetto. It was, you know, there wasn't this side, there was now in this side. They hear when we speak, we walk, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- now were you considered part of Germany, so you were forbidden to speak Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because it was the Third Reich. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Lodz, they give right away, [INAUDIBLE], and it was the Third Reich. It means it belongs to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did most people know how to speak German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. My brother used to say, when you speak high Jewish, came out German. My brother was a comic. Oh, he's-- he was something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, even though your parents were older, they were able to stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents were able to stay in the ghetto till-- till near the end. They kept working.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, kept working.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if your mother wasn't well?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wasn't old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were they at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was maybe 53, maybe. My mother was three, four years older. They wasn't very-- very old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if your mother wasn't looking too well, you'd hide her during the selections.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we'd hide her, oh, yeah. She got something on her face. It's-- I don't know how you call it. That's why we were scared they would take her right away. At least it was another year, I don't know how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the ghetto survived, but people kept being taken out. But the younger people, the people who were working, remained there until about August 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the liquidation.

speaker: interviewer
text: They liquidated the whole ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was-- I don't know. We still was in concentration camp nine months. We was three days in Auschwitz, and nine months we was in Bremen, and a week in Bergen-Belsen. Exactly to the week the-- the English soldier came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they liquidated the ghetto, everyone-- there was no selection? Everyone just had to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody has to leave. And the selection was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took my parents to the gas chamber. They took my sister-in-law and the little boy to the gas chamber, my mother, my father. Only me, my sister-- I was 18 years old, 17, 18. And my sister, she's five years, four years younger from me. So they took us to camp. And the rest, in Auschwitz, all to crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brother? Was he on this transport with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother, my sister-in-law, there was a different transport-- transport. So when you-- on this transport-- It was only my sister and my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was Rumkowski on this transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not on-- in this transport, he wasn't with us on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: But everyone was being taken out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- do you know how many days it took to take everyone out on the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Oh, we travel to-- to Auschwitz three days. That's in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three days--

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz is in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were given no food, no water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no it was open like the you-- horses, like animals, everything was open. And the bombs was flying. We wasn't scared [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any sign that the Russians were getting closer? Is that why the ghetto was liquidated then?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we live in the ghetto, and we was imagining we hear they are fighting. I don't know. We hear something. No, I think it was imagining. It was near Russia. That's why we could hear something. And we have radios. And we know when they catch you with a radio, whew, that was the worst-- the worst-- the worst what you can do. They kill you right away. No, the radios was under-- underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you sort of had an idea of what was happening in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know a little bit of what is going on through the radios. That was-- oh boy, you took a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: To listen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, if they catch, right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they were taking everyone out of the ghetto and just leaving a couple hundred people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left a few hundred. I don't know how-- how many. They left to clean up the ghetto because it's-- the people they left a lot of jewelry. And after the people-- they make the graves and they have to be shot and to fall to the graves. And the Russian came. And after we-- we saw them because I saw a few in Montreal. Mostly the people that was working, you know, the hard people working-- flour or potatoes, they was carrying on the-- the strong people-- they left them in the ghetto with their families.

speaker: interviewer
text: With their families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they killed them all before the Russians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't get killed because the Russian come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't manage to kill them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had dug the graves, but they didn't have a chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: The graves, and yeah, and not-- not one got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were taken by surprise.

speaker: interviewee
text: They called them the white workers. Yeah, a surprise. They didn't-- they didn't-- it was too late. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go now-- that you were-- you were on a train for three days. And you're getting out in Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the last time you saw your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was during the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: I look out through the window and I saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an--

speaker: interviewee
text: They put my mother separate and the men. They were all separate. They didn't put them together to kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea what was happening to them? Did you know at that point what it meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting on a cot. They picked-- they picked us to send out to Germany to work. We were sitting the whole night. We was freezing. And we sit together back by back. And they-- and from Czech, they came the first. They build Auschwitz. And a girl said to us, listen, if they're going to give you a towel, it means they send you to the crematorium. If they're not going to give you nothing, only a dress, they will send you to work. And we were sitting the whole night waiting for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't know what was going to happen in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people they send to-- to the gas chamber, they give towels. They shouldn't know nothing. It means they go to a bath, to a shower or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, we was waiting the whole day for the train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the train came. It was a lot to Hamburg, a lot to Bremen. It was a very big train. You know, they cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was-- you only were in Auschwitz for three days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your head shaved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, you went through that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they took away everything. They give you wooden shoes, and they give you only a dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't have a number there because you were there such a short time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the three days, not the three days, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they put you on trains again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were sitting the whole day. Next day, after the shower, we were sitting the whole day to wait for the train. And they give us soup. It was five-- five girls were sitting like that. The first-- no spoon. Take a little bit. You give the other one, the third one. It was the whole day what we got. We was waiting for the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my sister. Always with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone else there from Lodz that you knew, or was--

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them was from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were together with the girls from Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all of them from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were then put on trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were all--

speaker: interviewee
text: Open, open trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, open trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to Bremen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sent to Bremen. Were most of the girls in this group from Lodz sent with you to Bremen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all-- all of them from Lodz. Or they couldn't send them in one camp. They sent them to Czechoslovakia . They sent-- we came to Bremen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your group went to Bremen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. It was a port-- a port city.
